front: Opera: a singspiel premiered at the theater of its librettist Emanuel Schikaneder in Vienna, 1791; contains Masonic symbolism and is based on Jean Terrason's The Life of Sethos
Opera: Josepha Hofer (Aloysia Weber's sister) debuted the role of the Queen of the Night in the premiere; Emanuel Schikaneder debuted Papageno and was also the librettist
Opera: plot follows Tamino on a quest to rescue Pamina from Sarastro (who turns out not to be evil), aided by the title instrument and the comic birdcatcher Papageno with his magic bells
Opera: the title instrument allows its bearers to pass through chambers of fire and water (the Men in Armour); it can change sorrow into joy and summon wild animals to dance
Opera: Act I quintet in which several characters can only sing 'Hm! Hm! Hm! Hm!' after their mouths are padlocked to prevent lying
Opera: a 2004 Met production by Julie Taymor drew on Indonesian shadow puppet theater and Japanese bunraku
Opera: Act II opens in a palm grove where Sarastro invokes the gods Isis and Osiris
Opera aria: the Queen of the Night must reach high F6; covers two octaves (F4 to F6); sung when Pamina refuses her mother's order to murder Sarastro
back: "Der Hölle Rache" from The Magic Flute (Mozart)
work: "Der Hölle Rache" (Queen of the Night Aria)

front: Opera aria: coloratura passage beginning with a turn on A, eight eighth notes on high C, then descending a fifth to F — the passage is repeated down a third
back: "Der Hölle Rache" from The Magic Flute (Mozart)
work: "Der Hölle Rache" (Queen of the Night Aria)

front: Opera character: comic birdcatcher who accompanies the hero; typically costumed with colorful bird-like attire; uses magic bells to make the villain Monostatos dance away and later to summon his girlfriend
Opera character: role debuted by the librettist Emanuel Schikaneder; Mozart often doubled this character's vocal lines in the orchestra because Schikaneder was not a skilled singer
Opera aria: in 'In diesen heil'gen Hallen,' Sarastro forgives the woman who was ordered to stab him
back: Sarastro's aria from The Magic Flute (Mozart)
